steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which choice most logically completes the team's conclusion. The key evidence is that people across ten groups with different modes of living — urban, agricultural, and hunter-gatherer — rated odors about the same. The conclusion must follow directly from this cross-group consistency.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘culture likely plays more of a role in a person's perception of how pleasant an odor is than does human anatomy’: The data shows consistent ratings across cultures, which actually argues against culture being the dominant driver. This conclusion points in the wrong direction. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘a person's mode of living likely doesn't have a large influence on that person's perception of whether an odor is pleasant or unpleasant’: If urban dwellers, agricultural groups, and hunter-gatherers all rate odors similarly, then mode of living is not significantly shaping odor perception. This follows directly from the evidence. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘a person who lives in an urban area is more likely to encounter the odor of vanillin than is a person who lives in a small community’: The passage gives no data about how often people encounter specific odors. This claim is unrelated to what the study measured. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘a person who perceives certain odors as pleasant will likely perceive the odors as roughly equal in pleasantness’: This misreads the data. The passage shows vanillin was rated more pleasant than galbazine — two odors rated differently, not equally. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
